candidate_problems.text: What principled criterion distinguishes the mathematically explored structures that are genuinely more likely to be physically instantiated from the vast remainder? The conjecture appeals to 'strong load-bearing constraint,' but this is still qualitative. A worthwhile problem is to make that notion precise enough to generate risky predictions about which kinds of mathematics should recur in successful physics, rather than merely redescribing past successes.

Why does physical reality exhibit stable, compressible regularity at all, and are there deeper explanatory constraints on the existence, scope, or scale-dependence of such regularity? The conjecture relocates the mystery from mathematics to reality, but leaves open the central ontological question of why the world is lawful enough for mathematical compression. This is highly worth pursuing because it is the main residual explanatory burden created by the conjecture.

How can the conjecture avoid becoming selection-effect or hindsight reasoning? If mathematics is broad enough, some overlap with physics may be inevitable after the fact. The open problem is to formulate the view so that it explains not just that some mathematics applies, but why the applicable parts are unusually deep, unifying, and predictively fertile. Without this, the conjecture risks being too weak to distinguish genuine explanation from retrospective pattern-matching.
